Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Friston, Hohwy & Clark

contemporary

The predictive-processing constellation: free energy, active inference, and the embodied mind.

Karl Friston, Jakob Hohwy, and Andy Clark are best treated as three major nodes in the predictive-processing/free-energy tradition rather than as exponents of a single, fully unified theory of consciousness. Friston supplies the most general mathematical and neuroscientific architecture: the free-energy principle, active inference, Markov blankets, precision-weighted prediction error, and self-organization. Hohwy gives the most explicitly internalist, epistemological, and consciousness-focused philosophical development of predictive processing. Clark gives the most embodied, action-oriented, and anti-skull-bound interpretation, connecting predictive processing to the extended-mind and 4E cognition traditions.

The shared core is this: the mind is not a passive receiver of sensory givens. It is an active, hierarchically organized inferential system that predicts the causes of its sensory input, minimizes prediction error or variational free energy, controls the precision of error signals, and acts on the world to sample or produce the sensory evidence expected under its model. Friston’s 2010 formulation presents the free-energy principle as a unifying framework for action, perception, and learning, where global brain theories converge around the optimization of value, surprise, prediction error, or expected cost.

For consciousness, the common proposal is not simply “consciousness is prediction.” A more exact formulation is: conscious experience is a special regime of active inference involving hierarchical generative models, precision control, interoceptive bodily regulation, counterfactual action-readiness, and, in sophisticated agents, metacognitive access to the appearance/reality distinction. Hohwy and Seth explicitly warn that predictive processing is not itself, at the outset, a full theory of consciousness; rather, it can provide a systematic foundation for mapping neural substrates to phenomenological structure.

The resulting philosophy of mind is a form of active-inferential naturalism. It is anti-Cartesian, anti-sense-datum, and anti-raw-qualia. It is broadly physicalist or naturalistic, but not reductive in the old behaviorist sense. It treats mind as embodied and world-involving in Clark’s version, more inferentially secluded and skull-bound in Hohwy’s version, and formally grounded in self-organizing Markov-blanketed systems in Friston’s version. Its deepest tension is therefore this: predictive processing simultaneously supports a powerful internalist picture of mind as model-based inference and a powerful embodied picture of mind as action-oriented worldly engagement.

2. The historical position: from Cartesian theater to active inference

Historically, this tradition belongs to a long anti-passivist line in philosophy of mind. Against classical empiricism and sense-datum theories, it denies that experience begins with immediately given, epistemically basic sensory items. Against Cartesian dualism, it denies that consciousness requires a nonphysical mental substance. Against simple input-output computationalism, it denies that perception is a feed-forward registration of the world. Against purely linguistic or behaviorist approaches, it insists on a neurocomputational story of conscious content.

The positive lineage runs from Kant and Helmholtz through cybernetics, control theory, Bayesian brain theory, predictive coding, and contemporary computational neuroscience. Hohwy’s recent metaphysical work explicitly situates predictive processing in a line going “at least as far” as earlier optics and cognition, with a crucial modern transition from Kant to Helmholtz; he interprets predictive processing as a structural-realist descendant of Helmholtzian unconscious inference rather than as a form of transcendental idealism.

The Helmholtzian point is decisive. Perception is not a transparent window onto reality. It is an inference to the hidden causes of sensory stimulation. The brain does not merely receive “redness,” “pain,” “distance,” or “objecthood”; it infers hidden causes that best explain the sensory flux. Friston’s glossary definition of a generative model captures this: a generative model is a probabilistic model of dependencies between causes and consequences, with likelihoods and priors over causes. The Bayesian-brain hypothesis, in this setting, says that the brain uses internal probabilistic models to update posterior beliefs from sensory information in an approximately Bayes-optimal way.

Within twentieth- and twenty-first-century philosophy of mind, the Friston-Hohwy-Clark constellation intersects with several major debates.

First, it gives a new computational-neuroscientific version of representationalism: conscious contents represent inferred causes, but representation is not a static map; it is a temporally deep, action-guiding, uncertainty-regulating model.

Second, it transforms functionalism: mental states are not merely causal roles in an abstract machine, but precision-weighted, embodied, self-evidencing roles in an organism’s active regulation of itself.

Third, it engages with Dennett-style illusionism without simply accepting eliminativism. Friston, Clark, and Wilkinson’s “Bayesing Qualia” denies raw qualia as intrinsic givens, but it does not deny that there are distinctive sensory states. It redescribes them as inferred, mid-level latent variables held with unusual certainty.

Fourth, it is a major interlocutor for consciousness science. Hohwy has argued that prediction-error minimization can connect Bayesian metacognition with global neuronal workspace theory and can help explain the selection of representational content into consciousness.

Fifth, it is one of the most important contemporary attempts to reconcile neuroscience with the 4E tradition: embodied, embedded, enactive, and extended cognition. But this reconciliation is unstable. Hohwy thinks predictive processing reveals an evidentiary boundary and a skull-bound, inferentially secluded mind; Clark thinks predictive brains can be entry-points into larger embodied and extended cognitive circuits.

3. The shared machinery: generative models, prediction error, precision, action

The basic architecture is hierarchical Bayesian inference. A system has a generative model of the hidden causes of its sensory input. Higher levels predict lower-level states; lower levels send back prediction errors when the sensory flow diverges from expectation. Perception consists in minimizing those errors by updating the model. Action consists in minimizing prediction error by changing the sensory input itself.

Friston’s formulation of variational free energy is crucial here. Free energy is an information-theoretic quantity that bounds surprise: it is greater than, and therefore limits, the surprise associated with sampling data under a generative model. Since organisms cannot directly compute the true surprise of their sensory states without already knowing the true causes of those states, they minimize an accessible bound on surprise. This gives a formal account of how perception, learning, and action can be treated under one principle.

Three ideas matter especially for consciousness.

The first is precision. Prediction errors are not all equal. They are weighted by estimated reliability, confidence, or inverse uncertainty. Precision weighting determines which errors matter, which channels dominate, and which contents become salient. Hohwy’s 2012 account states the point directly: conscious perception can be understood as an outcome of prediction-error minimization, while attention is optimization of precision expectations during perceptual inference.

The second is active inference. The organism does not merely update beliefs to fit the world; it also acts to make the world fit its predictions, or to sample the world in ways that reduce uncertainty. In Friston’s 2010 framework, action suppresses sensory prediction errors that depend on expected movement trajectories, and motor control becomes action enslaved by proprioceptive prediction.

The third is temporal and counterfactual depth. A thermostat, reflex arc, or simple organism may minimize error, but consciousness plausibly requires a model capable of entertaining multiple possible futures, policies, bodily consequences, and appearance/reality contrasts. Friston, Wiese, and Hobson emphasize that more complex systems can be described as having beliefs about future trajectories or policies; systems with longer critical times look as if they pursue longer-term plans.

These three ingredients—precision, action, and temporal depth—are the bridge from predictive coding as a theory of perception to predictive processing as a theory of conscious mindedness.

4. Friston: free energy, Markov blankets, sentience, and Markovian monism

Friston’s importance is foundational. He is not primarily a philosopher of consciousness, but his free-energy principle has become one of the major formal frameworks through which philosophers and neuroscientists now discuss perception, action, selfhood, affect, and consciousness.

The free-energy principle starts from self-organization. Living systems persist by occupying a limited range of characteristic states. They do not dissipate into environmental disorder because they maintain their internal states within viable bounds. Formally, such systems can be described as minimizing variational free energy, which bounds sensory surprise. This is not simply a psychological theory; it is an attempt to express the conditions under which self-organizing systems remain the kinds of systems they are.

The Markov blanket is the crucial ontological device. A Markov blanket partitions a system into internal states, external states, and blanket states. Blanket states are divided into sensory and active states. Internal and external states do not directly influence each other; they interact through sensory and active blanket states. Friston, Wiese, and Hobson use this structure to define what it is for something to be a “thing” in a formal, dynamical sense.

This leads to what they call Markovian monism. The view is monist because there is fundamentally one kind of thing and one kind of irreducible property, not a separate mental substance. But one and the same Markov-blanketed system can be described from two perspectives: as a random dynamical system, and as a system whose internal states parameterize probabilistic beliefs about external states.

This is one of the deepest philosophical moves in Friston’s framework. It does not say that matter magically becomes mind. It says that under certain formal conditions, the same physical dynamics can be described intrinsically as physical state evolution and extrinsically as belief-like inference. The mental vocabulary enters not as a second substance but as a lawful, abstract, explanatory description of certain self-organizing systems.

Friston, Wiese, and Hobson are careful not to equate all Markov-blanketed systems with consciousness. They distinguish sentience in a minimal sense—responsiveness to sensory impressions—from phenomenal consciousness in the philosophical sense. Sentience, in their technical usage, only requires a non-empty subset of sensory states; phenomenal consciousness requires more.

Their route from sentience to consciousness is gradualist. Consciousness is not introduced by an ontological spark. It arises, if it arises, from increasing complexity in generative models: temporal depth, counterfactual trajectories, hierarchical Markov blankets within Markov blankets, and beliefs about beliefs. They explicitly suggest that consciousness is a vague concept with borderline cases, and that the hard-problem intuition may partly arise because vague gradual differences are mistaken for a metaphysical gap.

This is Fristonian physicalism, but not simple reductionism. It is reductive in the sense that no additional nonphysical property is required. It is nontrivial in the sense that mental descriptions are macroscopic, model-based, and explanatory. Friston, Wiese, and Hobson say that Markovian monism can ground representationalism and computational functionalism while remaining continuous with the dynamics of self-organizing life.

A second Fristonian strand, developed with Mark Solms, places affect and interoception at the center of consciousness. Solms and Friston argue that consciousness has causal powers and that its underlying function is free-energy minimization. They propose a three-level taxonomy: affective consciousness as arousal, perceptual consciousness as attentional activation of representations, and cognitive consciousness as reflective re-representation.

This affective interpretation matters because it prevents the predictive-processing theory from becoming too visual, cortical, or representational in a narrow sense. Consciousness is not only seeing objects. It is also being a living body whose needs, errors, drives, and regulatory demands are felt. In Solms and Friston’s formulation, affect registers deviations from preferred states and the directionality of work required to restore them. Precision-weighted prediction error is linked to reliability, confidence, and the impact of sensory evidence on internal expectations.

Friston’s own contribution to philosophy of mind can therefore be summarized as follows: mind is not an extra substance, but the inferential aspect of self-organizing, Markov-blanketed dynamics. Consciousness is not guaranteed by inference alone; it requires special forms of temporally deep, hierarchical, precision-controlled, interoceptively regulated active inference.

5. Hohwy: the predictive mind, the evidentiary boundary, and conscious self-evidencing

Hohwy is the most systematic philosopher of the predictive-processing view as a theory of mind. In The Predictive Mind, he presents the brain as a hypothesis-testing mechanism that minimizes prediction error about sensory input. The theory, he argues, explains the rich character of conscious perception, the role of attention, the dependence of perception on action, and the mind’s “fragile and indirect” relation to the world.

Hohwy’s distinctive move is internalist. If the brain minimizes prediction error over sensory input, then its evidence is at the sensory boundary. The brain does not have direct access to external causes; it infers them. In “The Self-Evidencing Brain,” he argues that prediction-error minimization implies an evidentiary boundary between brain and environment. This makes the mind more inferentially secluded and neurocentrically skull-bound than many embodied or enactive theorists would like, even though the framework can still accommodate some cases that motivate embodiment and enactivism.

This is not Cartesian dualism. Hohwy is not saying that the mind is immaterial or that the external world is unknowable. He is saying that the mind’s relation to the world is mediated by an internal generative model. In his 2025 metaphysical account, he defends this as an internalist, indirect conception of perception that remains compatible with realism, naturalism, and the claim that brains really implement predictive processing. He interprets the result as structural realism: perception gives us access to mind-independent structure, not to unconceptualized intrinsic reality.

For consciousness, Hohwy’s most important idea is conscious self-evidencing. A self-evidencing system acts to maximize evidence for its own existence as the kind of system it is. But Hohwy explicitly says this is not sufficient for consciousness in general. Many self-organizing systems self-evidence without being conscious. The task is to identify specific forms of self-evidencing that explain specific phenomenological properties.

This gives Hohwy’s theory a useful modesty. It does not pretend to solve the hard problem outright. It builds a map between phenomenological features and inferential mechanisms. Conscious perception is explained through hierarchical generative models updated by precision-weighted prediction errors. Active inference explains selective sampling and targeted testing of predictions. This can illuminate binocular rivalry, Troxler fading, and other paradigms in consciousness science.

Hohwy’s account of metacognition is especially important. Precision optimization requires monitoring uncertainty. Higher-level processes model the reliability of lower-level states and control the gain on prediction errors. This “constant hum of internal monitoring,” as Hohwy describes it, may underwrite introspection-like consciousness of one’s own mental representations.

His account of selfhood is similarly inferential. The self is not directly given. It is inferred as a hidden cause of sensory, interoceptive, proprioceptive, and action-related regularities. Stable traits and self-models arise only in systems with sufficient hierarchical and counterfactual depth. Hohwy even speculates that the minimal “sense of being” could be tied to a high-level precision of self-evidencing: a confidence-bearing form of the belief “I exist.”

Hohwy’s philosophy of mind is therefore a predictive-inferential internalism. Consciousness is world-directed, but it is formed through internal modeling. It unfolds in causal interaction with the world, but it is “forged” in detached self-evidencing processes.

The historical importance of Hohwy’s position is that it revives indirect realism in a neuroscientifically sophisticated form. Unlike old sense-datum theories, it does not posit private objects of awareness. Unlike naive direct realism, it denies unmediated perceptual access. Unlike idealism, it preserves mind-independent structure. The mind is not trapped in a private theater, but it is also not transparently open to the world. It is a controlled, self-evidencing inferential system.

6. Clark: predictive brains, embodied action, and generative entanglement

Clark accepts the predictive-processing architecture but resists Hohwy’s internalist conclusion. For Clark, predictive processing is not a reason to return to a skull-bound theory of mind. It is the best way to understand how embodied agents engage the world.

In “Whatever Next?”, Clark presents predictive processing as a unifying model of perception and action, one that illuminates attention and cortical processing and may offer the best clue to a unified science of mind and action. In Surfing Uncertainty, he stresses that predictive processing gives embodied cognitive science the principled neural story it previously lacked. The brain is a multi-layer probabilistic prediction machine, but that machine is designed for action, bodily engagement, and environmental coupling.

Clark’s dispute with Hohwy is clearest in “Busting Out.” Hohwy’s evidentiary-boundary argument suggests that predictive processing supports neurocentric internalism. Clark rejects that inference. Predictive brains, he argues, can participate in larger cognitive circuits; embodiment and action can contribute constitutively to knowing contact with the world.

This gives Clark’s version a more pragmatist and 4E character. The brain does not predict in order to contemplate an inner model. It predicts in order to act. Perception is not detached representation first and action-guidance second. Perception is for action all the way down. In “Beyond the Bayesian Blur,” Clark addresses the puzzle that probabilistic processing seems to yield determinate experience. His answer is that perceptual determinacy is not mysterious once perception is understood as action-oriented: embodied agents need sufficiently determinate grip on the world to act.

Clark’s most direct contribution to consciousness is the idea of “generative entanglement.” Conscious experience, he argues, flows from predictive entanglements that mix predictions about the world, the body, and the agent’s own reactive dispositions. This is not a simple inner-representation theory. A conscious experience is not just a model of an object; it is a model of worldly affordances, bodily states, and possible reactions.

Interoception is central here. Clark’s “Expecting Ourselves” project proposes that much of the distinctive feel of conscious experience may depend on interoceptive predictions about bodily physiological states. Basic conscious experience emerges when embodied, interoceptively informed agents survey opportunities for action; reflective self-consciousness arises when agents model themselves and others in socially complex ways.

Clark’s relation to Dennett is also central. In “Strange Inversions,” Clark examines Dennett’s idea that many apparently intrinsic experiential properties are really effects of our own reactive dispositions projected outward. Properties such as sweetness, blueness, or cuteness seem like causes of experience, but they are better understood as shorthand for patterns of response rooted in information processing. Clark connects this inversion to Bayesian predictive brains.

So Clark’s philosophy of mind is embodied predictive pragmatism. The mind is inferential, but inference is practical and action-oriented. Experience is not raw inner presentation; it is a structured grip on action-relevant possibilities in a world encountered by a living body.

7. Qualia: from raw givens to inferred latent variables

The most explicit joint treatment of consciousness by members of this constellation is Friston, Clark, and Wilkinson’s “Bayesing Qualia.” Its target is the hard/meta-hard problem: why do we think there is an explanatory gap between physical processes and qualitative experience?

Their core claim is subtle. They do not simply say qualia do not exist. They say qualia do not exist as raw, intrinsic, epistemically immediate givens. Instead, seeing red, feeling pain, and even having a sense of self are inferred causes posited by the generative model to explain sensory and bodily flux. These inferred causes are represented with especially high certainty, and that certainty generates the Cartesian temptation: the feeling that experience is indubitable, intrinsic, and metaphysically special.

Precision is the key mechanism. Attention increases the precision, confidence, or certainty assigned to some contents. The agent need not know how these precision assignments are computed; they are opaque. The agent can then hold a mid-level sensory content fixed—“this looks red,” “this hurts,” “this sounds loud”—while varying top-level beliefs about the world: perhaps it is an illusion, a dream, a hallucination, a trick, or a misclassification.

This generates the appearance/reality gap. I can doubt whether there is a red object before me, but I cannot doubt, in the same way, that it looks red to me. The predictive-processing explanation is that “looking red” is a highly precise mid-level inferential posit, while “there is a red object” is a higher-level worldly hypothesis that can be varied. Qualia become “predictively potent mid-level latent variables,” not raw data.

The resulting view is neither standard qualia realism nor standard illusionism. It is realist in identifying qualia with distinctive mid-level sensory states known with high systemic certainty. It is illusionist in denying that these states are metaphysically special intrinsic simples. The illusion is not the existence of sensory experience; the illusion is the certainty and metaphysical interpretation attached to it.

The mature conscious agent, on this account, has three dissociable capacities: a temporally deep generative model of worldly states; responsiveness to interoceptive bodily information; and the capacity to hold mid-level sensory recodings fixed while imaginatively varying top-level beliefs. This is what allows the agent to understand the difference between how things appear and how they are.

This is probably the cleanest statement of the philosophy of consciousness extractable from the triad: qualia are not inputs to inference; they are outputs of inference. We do not infer that we have qualitative experiences because we first encounter raw redness or raw pain. Rather, we experience redness and pain because the generative system infers highly certain, action-relevant, body-involving latent causes.

8. The philosophy of mind extracted from the three thinkers

A comprehensive philosophy of mind can be extracted from Friston, Hohwy, and Clark, but it must preserve their tensions. I would formulate it as active-inferential embodied structural realism.

Its first thesis is anti-passivism. Mind is not a mirror receiving impressions. Perception, attention, action, and learning are active attempts to reduce uncertainty under a generative model. This places the view against classical empiricist “givenism” and against any feed-forward picture of cognition.

Its second thesis is inferential representationalism. Experience represents the hidden causes of sensory input, but representation is not a static inner picture. It is a probabilistic, hierarchical, action-sensitive model that is constantly updated through prediction error and precision control.

Its third thesis is active embodiment. Action is not merely an output after perception; it is part of inference. The agent samples the world to test and fulfill predictions. Clark stresses this most strongly, but it is already present in Friston’s active inference and in Hohwy’s account of selective sampling.

Its fourth thesis is precision-based attention. Attention is not a spotlight added to perception from outside. It is the optimization of precision: the weighting of prediction errors by expected reliability. Conscious content depends not only on what is predicted, but on how confidently, reliably, and action-relevantly it is encoded.

Its fifth thesis is interoceptive affectivism. Consciousness is not merely exteroceptive object perception. Bodily regulation, affect, arousal, and interoceptive prediction contribute to the felt character of experience. This is especially strong in Solms and Friston, Clark’s generative entanglement, and Friston-Clark-Wilkinson’s three-factor account of conscious agents.

Its sixth thesis is qualia revisionism. Qualia are neither raw mental simples nor eliminable fictions. They are inferred, high-precision, mid-level latent variables in a generative model of embodied engagement. Their apparent indubitability is explained by precision and metacognitive opacity.

Its seventh thesis is self-modeling. The self is not a Cartesian ego. It is an inferred hidden cause, a relatively stable structure within the generative model that explains patterns of interoception, proprioception, agency, memory, social interaction, and policy selection. Hohwy’s “sense of being” is the most austere version; Clark’s reflective self-consciousness is the more social and embodied version.

Its eighth thesis is graded consciousness. There is no single metaphysical switch from non-conscious matter to conscious mind. There are degrees and kinds of self-organization, temporal depth, interoceptive regulation, counterfactual policy space, metacognition, and hierarchical self-modeling. Friston, Wiese, and Hobson explicitly treat consciousness as vague and gradual, with possible borderline cases.

Its ninth thesis is naturalistic monism. There is no need for a second mental substance or irreducible phenomenal property. But mental descriptions are not dispensable either. They are high-level, model-based, macroscopic descriptions of physical systems whose internal states can be treated as encoding probabilistic beliefs. Friston’s Markovian monism gives the formal metaphysics; Hohwy’s structural realism gives the epistemology; Clark’s embodied pragmatism gives the action theory.

Its tenth thesis is meta-problem dissolution rather than hard-problem solution in the old sense. The theory does not produce a magical deduction of “what-it-is-likeness” from equations. It explains why creatures like us come to represent some of their own states as mysterious, certain, private, and detachable from the world. That is a powerful response to the meta-problem of consciousness, even if it does not satisfy those who demand an intrinsic-phenomenal explanation.

9. The central internal tension: Hohwy versus Clark

The most important philosophical tension is not between predictive processing and its critics, but inside predictive processing itself.

Hohwy’s version says: because the brain’s evidence is sensory input, the mind is inferentially secluded. The world is known through a generative model. The boundary between brain and world is epistemically decisive. This yields indirect realism, evidentiary boundaries, and a skull-bound conception of cognition.

Clark’s version says: predictive processing does not imply skull-bound mind. Predictive brains are deeply coupled to bodies and environments. They participate in larger cognitive circuits. Action and environmental structure are not mere external causes of inner cognition; they can be constitutive parts of cognitive success.

Friston’s framework can be used by both. Markov blankets define boundaries, which supports Hohwy. But Markov blankets can also be nested, multi-scale, and dynamically coupled, which supports Clark’s broader embodied interpretation. A person, a nervous system, a body, a dyad, a social group, or an organism-environment niche can be modeled at different scales, depending on the explanatory project.

The most charitable synthesis is this: Hohwy is right about the epistemic structure of perceptual inference; Clark is right about the ecological structure of cognition. The brain infers hidden causes from sensory evidence, but the inferential system is not functionally intelligible apart from bodily action, affective regulation, environmental affordances, and social scaffolding.

10. Neural correlates and empirical implications

Predictive processing is especially useful for consciousness science because it does not merely ask where consciousness is located. It asks what kind of computational role a neural process plays in a generative hierarchy.

The relevant neural questions become: Which prediction errors are precision-weighted? Which levels of the hierarchy stabilize conscious contents? Which forms of recurrent processing support counterfactual depth? Which interoceptive signals modulate perceptual experience? Which gain-control mechanisms implement attention? Which metacognitive processes allow the agent to report confidence, uncertainty, or appearance/reality distinctions?

Hohwy and Seth’s point is that predictive processing can improve the search for neural correlates because it provides systematic mappings between neural substrate and phenomenological structure, while remaining flexible enough not to be a single dogmatic theory of consciousness.

This also means that predictive processing can mediate between rival theories. Global workspace theory can be interpreted as a story about which contents become globally available under precision-weighted policy and action demands. Higher-order theories can be reinterpreted through Bayesian metacognition and second-order uncertainty monitoring. Affective/interoceptive theories can be integrated through bodily prediction and allostatic regulation. IIT is more difficult to integrate, because it begins from axioms about integrated information rather than from active inference and extrinsic information geometry, though Friston, Wiese, and Hobson note possible formal relations and differences.

11. Artificial consciousness, cautiously

Artificial consciousness is not central to Friston, Hohwy, and Clark’s joint philosophy of consciousness, but the free-energy framework has obvious implications for it.

The key implication is negative: merely simulating predictive processing may not be enough. A system would need more than a generative model in software. It would need some analogue of self-organization, active inference, embodied sensorimotor coupling, precision control, temporally deep policy selection, and perhaps interoceptive or homeostatic regulation. Recent FEP-based work on artificial consciousness argues that self-organizing systems such as organisms share properties that could in principle be realized artificially, but that classical von Neumann computers do not instantiate; one proposed distinction is between merely simulating and actually replicating consciousness through the right kind of causal flow.

Friston himself has been publicly cautious. In a reported interview, he said it would be “nice” if the free-energy principle led to true artificial consciousness someday, but that this was not one of his top priorities.

Clark’s recent relevance to AI is more likely to come through extended mind and human-AI cognitive coupling than through a claim that current AI systems are conscious. Hohwy’s relevance lies in demarcation: not every uncertainty-minimizing or generative system is conscious. On the extracted philosophy developed here, an artificial system would be a serious candidate for consciousness only if it were an autonomous, self-maintaining, embodied or functionally body-like active-inference system with temporally deep counterfactual modeling, precision-guided attention, interoceptive/homeostatic significance, and some form of metacognitive self-evidencing.

That is a much stronger requirement than language generation, behavioral fluency, or large-scale statistical pattern recognition.

12. Critical assessment

The first strength of the Friston-Hohwy-Clark constellation is unification. It connects perception, action, attention, learning, affect, selfhood, and consciousness under one formal family of ideas. Few contemporary frameworks have comparable reach.

The second strength is that it replaces sterile dichotomies. Perception is neither passive reception nor arbitrary construction. Qualia are neither magical intrinsic properties nor nothing at all. The self is neither a Cartesian ego nor a mere fiction. The body is neither an external input device nor the whole mind. Consciousness is neither a simple neural location nor an inexplicable metaphysical glow.

The third strength is that it offers a naturalistic explanation of why consciousness seems mysterious. The appearance/reality distinction, the opacity of precision assignments, the high certainty of mid-level sensory states, and the capacity to imagine skeptical scenarios together explain why agents like us infer a special realm of qualia.

The main weakness is over-generality. The free-energy principle can seem so broad that it risks explaining too much. If many self-organizing systems minimize free energy, then free-energy minimization alone cannot explain consciousness. The theory must add constraints: temporal depth, hierarchical structure, interoception, counterfactual policy space, precision control, metacognition, and self-modeling. But the exact threshold remains contested.

The second weakness is the hard problem. Hohwy is appropriately modest: self-evidencing helps explain properties of consciousness, but it is not a complete theory of consciousness. Friston, Clark, and Wilkinson offer a powerful account of the meta-hard problem—why qualia seem mysterious—but critics may say that explaining puzzlement about experience is not the same as explaining experience itself.

The third weakness is the internalist/embodied tension. Hohwy’s evidentiary veil and Clark’s embodied “busting out” cannot both be the whole story without qualification. The best synthesis distinguishes epistemic mediation from ecological constitution: sensory evidence is internally modeled, but cognitive success is often constituted by embodied world-involving loops.

The fourth weakness is empirical specificity. Precision, prediction error, and generative modeling are powerful concepts, but identifying their exact neural implementation in specific conscious states is difficult. Predictive processing is best seen as a research framework that constrains empirical theories, not as a finished neural map.

13. Final synthesis

Friston gives us the ontology and formal dynamics: organisms are self-organizing Markov-blanketed systems that minimize variational free energy and can be described as performing active inference. Hohwy gives us the epistemology and internal phenomenology: conscious minds are self-evidencing systems whose experience is formed through hierarchical internal modeling, precision control, and metacognitive monitoring. Clark gives us the embodied philosophy of agency: predictive brains are not detached spectators but action-oriented participants in bodily, worldly, and sometimes extended cognitive circuits.

The philosophy of mind that emerges is this:

Mind is active inference.
Perception is controlled, error-corrected hallucination constrained by the world.
Action is inference enacted.
Attention is precision control.
Affect is interoceptive regulation made phenomenologically significant.
Qualia are high-precision inferred latent variables, not raw givens.
The self is an inferred, temporally extended, embodied hidden cause.
Consciousness is graded, temporally deep, counterfactual, interoceptive, and metacognitively structured.
The metaphysics is naturalistic monism; the epistemology is structural realism; the agency theory is embodied pragmatism.

Historically, this is one of the most ambitious contemporary successors to Helmholtzian unconscious inference, cybernetic control theory, functionalism, representationalism, and Dennettian anti-Cartesianism. It is also one of the most serious current attempts to put philosophy of mind and neuroscience into a single explanatory architecture. Its central promise is that it makes consciousness scientifically tractable without trivializing its phenomenological richness. Its central unresolved problem is whether explaining the inferential structure and meta-problem of experience is enough to explain why experience exists at all.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
